ground_truth.title: Egyptian Hieroglyphs: Lesson 1
ground_truth.main_content: Egyptian Hieroglyphs: Lesson 1 - General Overview. The ancient Egyptians created a highly flexible hieroglyphic system of writing. Hieroglyphs could be arranged in both columns and rows and could be read from the left or from the right, depending on how they were written. This allowed the ancient Egyptians to effortlessly integrate their writing with art, blurring the boundary between art and script. In the example below, the god Amun, imn, is written each of the possible combinations. Reading Hieroglyphs: The god Amun is shown with horizontal and vertical arrangements, reading left to right and right to left. The key to reading direction is to look for a hieroglyph with a face and read toward it. When the figure is facing to the left, begin reading from the left. If they are facing right, begin from the right. When hieroglyphs are stacked on top of each other, the top sign should always be read before lower sign. Another feature is group writing where hieroglyphs were arranged to reduce empty space: taller signs stand alone, while smaller signs are stacked on top of each other. Transliteration and the Alphabet: Hieroglyphs representing single consonants are called uniliterals. The alphabet includes: Vulture (3), Reed leaf (i/j), Double reed leaf (y), Arm (ʿ), Quail chick/w (w), Foot (b), Stool (p), Horned viper (f), Owl (m), Water/n (n), Mouth (r), House plan (h), Rope (ḥ), Unknown (ḫ), Animal belly (ẖ), Door bolt (z), Folded cloth (s), Pool (š), Hill (q), Basket (k), Stand for vessel (g), Bread loaf (t), Tethering rope (ṯ), Hand (d), Cobra (ḏ). The ancient Egyptian language also contains biliterals and triliterals representing two and three consonants. Pronunciation: Hieroglyphic script omitted vowels, making pronunciation challenging. The general convention is to add an 'e' between consonants. Examples: 3 (ah), i/j (ee), w (oo), b (b), f (f), m (m), n (n), r (r), h (h), ḥ (h), ḫ (kh), ẖ (kyah), z (s), s (s), š (sh), q (k), k (k), g (g), t (t), ṯ (tch), d (d), ḏ (dj). Phonograms, Ideograms, and Determinatives: Hieroglyphs are visual representations of objects or ideas. When used to represent real world things, they are called ideograms. Phonograms represent specific sounds and allow scribes to spell words. Determinatives are placed at the end of words and provide general meaning without phonetic value. For example, the face glyph (ḥr) represents 'face' as an ideogram, but provides phonetic value when used as a phonogram. The leg determinative indicates movement-related concepts. Memorization: Biliterals include ir, mi, mn, nb, ḥr, sš. Triliterals include ʿnḫ, nfr, nṯr. Exercises: Four exercises test reading direction and glyph order identification with answers provided in toggle sections.
with: The ancient Egyptians created a highly flexible hieroglyphic system of writing.
Look for a hieroglyph with a face and read toward it.
Hieroglyphs in the alphabet are called uniliterals because they represent a single consonant.
Phonograms are hieroglyphs that represent a specific sound (phonetic value).
A determinative is a hieroglyph that does not have a phonetic value so it is not transliterated.
without: Return to Index – Continue to Lesson 2
